musicalLanguage.description: Weeping Willow (1903) is one of Joplin's slower, more deliberately unhurried rags. The harness's key-detection pass confirms the stated C major strongly (statedKeyFit 0.803), and the confidence-gated windows spell a clean C-major vocabulary — but the single most-cited label is G, the dominant (15% of windows considered), well ahead of the C tonic itself (8%), alongside F as IV (8%), D7 as a secondary dominant (7%) resolving to G, and Am7 as vi7 (4%). A piece that names its own dominant almost twice as often as its tonic is leaning hard on V-to-I motion rather than sitting still on I. At 148 measures and 70 BPM in 2/4, the piece's back half (measures 84-115) alternates busy and sparse measures roughly every eight bars — a genuine antiphonal, call-and-response texture rather than a steady density.
musicalLanguage.structure: An A strain (measures 4-36, an 8-measure cell recurring at measures 5, 13, 21, 29) restates in full at measures 68-84, establishing the G-C-F progression (dominant, tonic, subdominant). A middle section (measures 37-67) introduces D7-G7 secondary-dominant turnarounds. The back half (measures 84-130) alternates a dense right-hand cell (measures 85-90, 93-99, 101-106, 109-115, graded strong with a 3.57 compression ratio) against comparatively sparse neighboring bars (84, 92, 108), before a closing section (measures 131-148) resolves to a final C cadence.
keyMoments: The G dominant accounts for 15% of chord windows considered, nearly double the C tonic's own 8% — a piece that spends more labeled time announcing its dominant than sitting on its tonic, an unusual balance worth listening for directly.

Measures 85, 93, 101, and 109 (each 12 onsets) alternate with sparser neighbors at 84, 92, and 108 (4-5 onsets) — a genuine call-and-response density pattern across the piece's back half, not a steady buildup.
Measures 4-20 restate in full at measures 68-84 — the pattern pass grades this an 83-interval, strong right-hand match, confirming a literal A-strain repeat.

The D7-to-G secondary-dominant motion (windows at measures 37, 45, 53, 61) recurs four times in the middle section alone — a single harmonic gesture worth isolating and drilling on its own before attempting the full passage.
teachingGoals: Learn the C-major tonic-IV-dominant vocabulary (C-F-G) that anchors this piece, confirmed at a strong 0.803 fit — but notice the dominant (G) actually outweighs the tonic in label frequency, and let your ear register that emphasis.

Practice alternating between dense and sparse measures with real dynamic contrast (measures 85 versus 84, 93 versus 92) — the call-and-response texture in the back half is a written instruction, not incidental.
Recognize a literal strain repeat (measures 4-20 restating at 68-84) and use it to halve your memorization work — once the first statement is secure, the second needs only review.
Practice playing a rag slowly, at 70 BPM, without letting the rhythm go slack — the tempo removes speed as a source of energy, so the syncopation has to carry the momentum instead.
styleTips: Listen for the G dominant landing solidly and often — it is this piece's most repeated single harmonic label, more common than the C tonic itself, and a clear ear for it makes the eventual tonic resolutions land harder.
Play the alternating dense and sparse measures (85 vs. 84, 93 vs. 92) with a visible give and take in volume — treat it as a real musical conversation between phrases, not uniform playing.
Feel the D7 pulling toward G each time it recurs (measures 37, 45, 53, 61) — repetition of a secondary dominant is a chance to make the resolution more expressive each time, not just correct.
Try a slow, unhurried practice tempo even once the notes are secure — at 70 BPM, rushing is the easiest way to lose Weeping Willow's gentler character.
